coredata.dc:title: Reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite as an enhanced material for electrochemical determination of dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shirong
Huang, Qitong
Lin, Yi
Wei, Chan
Zhang, Hanqiang
Zhang, Wuxiang
Guo, Zhenbo
Bao, Xiuxiu
Shi, Jiangu
Hao, Aiyou
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed and characterized a new dopamine (DA) sensor based on the reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-carbon dot composite film with high sensitivity, nice specificity and good stability. The carbon dots (CDs) had carboxyl groups with negative charge, which not only provided good stability, but also enabled interaction with amine functional groups in DA through electrostatic interaction to enhance the specificity of DA with high specificity, and the interaction and electron communication between rGO and DA can be further strengthened via π-π stacking force. Under optimal conditions, the rGO-CDs electrode (GCE) showed better electrochemical response towards the detection of DA than the bare GCE, GO/GCE and CDs/GCE. A linear relationship between the oxidation peak current of DA and its concentration could be obtained in a range from 0.01000μM to 450.0μM with the limit of detection as 1.5nM (3S/N). Furthermore, rGO-CDs/GCE exhibited good ability to suppress the background current from large excess ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA). Meanwhile, the rGO-CDs/GCE also was applied to the detection of DA content in the injection of DA with satisfactory results, and the biosensor could keep its activity for at least a month.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Carbon dots
Dopamine
Electrochemistry
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GO, CDs, and rGO-CDs/GCE
Cyclic voltammetric behavior of DA on the rGO-CDs/GCE
Calibration plot and limit of detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of GO (B) SEM image of rGO-CDs (C) TEM image of CDs (D) UV-vis absorption spectra of GO, CDs, and rGO-CDs intensity.
CV of 0.10mM DA recorded on bare GCE, GO/GCE, CDs/GCE and rGO-CDs/GCE in the pH 6.5 phosphate buffer solution (scan rate: 1.0V s−1).
CVs of 0.15mM DA on rGO-CDs/GCE in 0.02M phosphate buffer solutions with various pH values (a-l: 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5).
CV of 0.10mM DA on rGO-CDs/GCE in the pH 6.5 phosphate buffer solution at various scan rates (a-m: 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.075, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50V s−1).
CV of 0.15mM DA with the different concentrations of AA (A): 0mM (a), 0.15mM (b), 1.0mM (c), 15.0mM (d); and UA (B): 0mM (a), 0.15mM (b), 1.0mM (c), 15.0mM (d).
Results of determination of DA in its injection.

head.ce:title: Reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite as an enhanced material for electrochemical determination of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Environment Science, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed and characterized a new dopamine (DA) sensor based on the reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-carbon dot composite film with high sensitivity, nice specificity and good stability. The carbon dots (CDs) had carboxyl groups with negative charge, which not only provided good stability, but also enabled interaction with amine functional groups in DA through electrostatic interaction to enhance the specificity of DA with high specificity, and the interaction and electron communication between rGO and DA can be further strengthened via π-π stacking force. Under optimal conditions, the rGO-CDs electrode (GCE) showed better electrochemical response towards the detection of DA than the bare GCE, GO/GCE and CDs/GCE. A linear relationship between the oxidation peak current of DA and its concentration could be obtained in a range from 0.01000μM to 450.0μM with the limit of detection as 1.5nM (3S/N). Furthermore, rGO-CDs/GCE exhibited good ability to suppress the background current from large excess ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA). Meanwhile, the rGO-CDs/GCE also was applied to the detection of DA content in the injection of DA with satisfactory results, and the biosensor could keep its activity for at least a month.

ce:section.ce:para: Acting as a two-dimensional (2D) carbon material, graphene has shown its fascinating applications in bioelectronics and biosensing due to its unique physical and chemical properties, such as exceptional thermal, chemical and mechanical properties, high specific surface area and excellent conductivity [1–3]. Despite their unique electrocatalytic properties, graphene tend to irreversible agglomerate and precipitate in aqueous solutions due to strong π-π stacking or van der Waals interactions between individual graphene sheets [4–6], which limit its direct use for biological applications. As we know, quantum dots (QDs) are usually coated with organic ligands which can overcome their aggregation and exploit profit of their full capabilities [6]. Similar to CdSe QDs, Carbon dots (CDs) are novel ‘zero-dimensional’ carbon nanomaterials, which also could effectively prevent the aggregating of graphene. Compared with conventional QDs, CDs are superior in terms of low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, economical and remarkable conductivity [7–9]. Meanwhile, in this work, Carbon dots were as reducing agents [9,10], which can reduce the graphene oxide (GO) to reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Wang [4] had used the rGO decorated with carbon dots (rGO-CDs) to detect acetylcholine. Yang [11] had developed a sensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on CDs/graphene.

Dopamine (DA, 3,4-dihydroxyphenyl ethylamine), an important neurotransmitter of catecholamine class in the human brain, plays a significant role in the renal, cardiovascular, central nervous, and hormonal systems. Abnormalities in dopamine concentrations have been linked to several neurological disorders, for example, parkinsonism, epilepsy and schizophrenia [12–14]. In addition, DA is available as an intravenous medication, which acts on the sympathetic nervous system, to produce effects such as increasing heart rates and blood pressure [15]. Therefore, detection and quantification of DA in vivo/vitro is a subject of great importance in the clinical medical practice.

Up to now, electrochemical analytical techniques have attracted wide attention for DA determination due to their simplicity, low cost, high sensitivity and fast response [16–20]. However, on a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) the coexisting compounds such as uric acid (UA) or ascorbic acid (AA) in the biological samples can cause great interference due to the similar oxidation potential close to that of DA [20]. To resolve this problem, various graphene-based materials have been employed to modify the surface of working electrodes or as the electrode materials based on the π-π stacking interaction between phenyl moiety of DA and the long range π-conjugation of graphene, Such as graphene-chitosan [16], β-cyclodextrin/graphene [17], GO [18], polypyrrole/graphene [19]. Although most of these systems made many contributions to detection of dopamine, these modified materials either exhibited limited sensitivity or they involved sophisticated and expensive syntheses. In this work, a green and economical composite of rGO-CDs was utilized to construct a novel DA biosensor. The CDs can provide abundant carboxyl groups at surface which can significantly enhance the redox response of DA. In this way, the rGO-CDs composite not only provides an excellent environment for DA oxidation but also acts as a barrier to prevent AA and UA from interfering in the DA detection. In our work, selective detection of DA in a linear concentration range of 0.01000μM-450.0μM was obtained, which revealed a lower limit of detection and wider linear response compared to some previously reported DA electrochemical biosensors. Meanwhile, the rGO-CDs/GCE also was applied to the detection of DA content in the injection of DA with satisfactory results, and the biosensor could keep its activity for at least two weeks.

Glucose, ethanol, uric acid and dopamine were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), graphite powder, oleic acid, and ascorbic acid were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China), phosphate buffer was from Shanghai Kangyi Instrument Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical reagents. Nanopure deionized and distilled water (18.2MΩ) was used throughout all experiments.
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out on a CHI 650D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai ChenHua Instruments Co., China).

A conventional three-electrode system, consisting of a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, a Ag|AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode and and a bare or modified GCE as working electrode (of 3.0mm diameter), was used in all electrochemical experiments. All pH measurements were carried out on a pHS-3C exact digital pH metre (Shanghai Mettler-Toledo Instruments Co., Ltd), which was calibrated with standard pH buffers. The UV-visible absorption was registered on a Mapada UV-1800PC (Shanghai China). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed on a S-4800 electron microscope (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed on a JEM-1230 electron microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan) at 300kV. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

GO was prepared according to Hummer's method [21] and the CDs were prepared according to Chen's method [22]. rGO-CDs were prepared as follows: 1mg GO was added to 10mL CD solution (8.0mgmL−1). After that, the composite was placed into a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for 4h at 90°C, then the rGO-CDs was obtained.

The GCE was polished to a mirror-like surface using 1.0μm, 0.3μm, and 0.05μm γ-alumina slurry, respectively, and then washed successively with distilled water, ethanol and distilled water in an ultrasonic bath, and dried in air before use. Then with a micro injector, 5.0μL of the rGO-CDs mixture solution, 5.0μL of the GO solution and 5.0μL of the CDs solution were applied to the surface of GCE, respectively, and left to dry in an oven at 60°C for 30min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GO, CDs, and rGO-CDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of GO (Fig. 1
A) and rGO-CDs (Fig. 1 B) were characterized by SEM, the typically crumpled and wrinkled structure of GO and rGO-CDs was observed. Furthermore, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1 B, it is apparent that the graphene sheets were decorated with CDs having sizes ranging from 5 to 15nm. As presented in Fig. 1
C, the CDs was characterized by TEM with the sizes ranging from 2 to 10nm., Fig. 1 D showed the UV-Visible absorption spectra of GO, CDs and rGO-CDs. GO exhibited a well-defined absorption peak at 233nm; CDs provided a characteristic peak at 278nm. In contrast, There is no obvious absorption peak of the rGO-CDs ascribable to the CDs reacted with GO to produce rGO-CDs [4,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetric behavior of DA on the rGO-CDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of 0.2mM DA was carried out at a bare GCE, a CDs/GCE, a GO/GCE and rGO-CDs/GCE electrode in pH 6.5 phosphate buffer. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the cyclic voltammogram obtained at a bare GCE shows an oxidation peak current at -2.369μA and a reduction peak at 0.861μA. After immobilising CDs on the same electrode, the oxidation current and the reduction current were observed to have increased by 47.7% and 39.5%, respectively. On the GO/GCE, the oxidation current and the reduction current were observed to have increased by 33.8% and 83.5%, respectively. However, on a graphene-carbon dots modified glassy carbon electrode, the oxidation current and the reduction current further increased by 213.3% and 208.8%. A possible reaction mechanism of rGO-CDs/GCE with DA was discussed, the π-π stacking interaction between phenyl moiety of DA and the long range π-conjugation of rGO. The CDs could make the surface of the electrode more conductive, and the CDs had carboxyl groups with negative charge, which not only made it have good stability but also enables interaction with amine functional groups in DA through electrostatic interaction to multiply recognize DA with high specificity.

The electrochemical redox behaviors of DA at rGO-CDs/GCE in buffer systems with different pH values (range from 3.0 to 8.5) were investigated by CV and the result was shown in Fig. 3 . The oxidation peak potential shifted negatively with the increase of pH value, indicating that protons involved in the electrode reaction. A good linear relationship between E
pa and pH was constructed with linear regression equation as E
pa (V)=-0.0604 pH+0.766 (R=-0.992, n=5). The slope value of -60.4mV/pH shows that the electron transfer was accompanied by an equal number of protons. At the same time, when the pH value was 6.5, the oxidation peak current (I
pa ) of DA was the largest. Therefore, considering the sensitive determination for DA, the phosphate buffer of pH=6.5 was chosen as the optimal pH acidity in this experiment.

To investigate the reaction kinetics, Fig. 4 showed the CV of 0.200mM DA at rGO-CDs/GCE at different scan rate. With the increase of the scan rate, the redox peak currents increased simultaneously, accompanied with an enlargement of the peak separation. Moreover, both the cathodic and anodic peak currents increased with the scan rate from 0.01 to 5.0V s−1, which indicated that the electro-redox of DA on rGO-CDs/GCE was a typical diffusion controlled process. In addition, at higher scan rates, the anode (E
pa ) and cathode (E
pc ) peak potential have a linear relationship with the logarithm of scan rate (log v). The regression equations were E
pa (V) = 0.0911 logv (V s−1)+0.373 (R=0.994, n=5) and E
pc (V)=-0.0578 logv (V s−1)+0.0206 (R=0.995, n=5), respectively. The relationship between the potential and scan rate could be described as following equations by Laviron [24]:
(1)
E
p a
=
E
0 '
+
2.3 R T
( 1 − α ) n F
log ν
(2)
E
p c
=
E
0 '
−
2.3 R T
α n F
log ν
(3)
log
k s
= α log ( 1 − α ) + ( 1 − α ) log α − log
R T
n F v
−
( 1 − α ) α n F Δ
E p
2.3 R T

Where α is the electron transfer coefficient, n is the number of transfer electron, k
s is the standard heterogeneous rate constant, R, T and F have their usual significance. After making computations: α =0.56, n =1.93, k s =0.58 s−1. Which indicated that the electrochemical oxidation of DA at the rGO-CDs/GCE is a diffusion-controlled process and not a surface-controlled process [25,26].

It is well known that the oxidation peak potentials for ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and DA are very close to each other and thus it is difficult to separate these compounds due to their overlapping signals on a bare GCE. This problem can be eliminated by electrostatic attraction, since the rGO-CDs film, which is in its anionic form at the working electrode surface in the pH 6.5 phosphate buffer solution, both AA (pKa=4.1) and UA (pKa=5.75) are negatively charged, but DA (pK=8.89) is positively charged at physiological pH=6.5 [14]. Additionally, compare with AA and UA, DA has a special aromatic ring, therefore the interaction and electron communication between rGO and DA can be further strengthened via π-π stacking force. The CDs had carboxyl groups with negative charge, which could prevent anionic AA and UA from reaching the electrode surface. So, rGO-CDs, as a cationic exchanger at the GCE surface selectively attracts cationic DA and allows them to pass through to the electrode surface. Meanwhile, rGO-CDs prevents anionic AA and UA from reaching the electrode surface. As a result, AA and UA did not exchange electrons with the electrode. As shown in Fig. 5 A (AA) and Fig. 5 B (UA), it could be concluded that the presence of AA and UA did not interfere in the DA determination.

Furthermore, other influences from common co-existing substances were also investigated. When the relative error (Er) exceeded 5%, each matter was considered as an interfering agent. It was found that most ions and common substances at high concentration only caused negligible change: Na+, K+, Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2− (>300 fold), Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, (150 fold), lysine, cysteine and glucose (100 fold), The results indicated the rGO-CDs/GCE exhibited good selectivity for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration plot and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para:
By using the more sensitive differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) as the detection method, the rGO-CDs/GCE was further used for the DA detection. Under the optimal conditions, the oxidation peak current (I
pa ) of DA increased with its concentration in the range from 0.01000μM to 450.0μM with typical DPV shown in Fig. 6 , which was the linear regression equations as I pa (μA)=-0.00274 C (μM) - 0.284 (R=0.995, N=5). The detection limit was calculated as 1.5nM, which was lower than some previous reports [14–17], which illustrated rGO-CDs/GCE had good sensitivity and wide linear range. In addition, the biosensor could keep its activity for at least two weeks.

The utilization of the proposed method in real samples analysis was also investigated by direct analysis of DA in the injection solution of DA. We examined the ability in DPV determination of DA concentration in an injection solution based on the repeated DPV responses (n=5) of the diluted analytes and the samples that were spiked with specified concentration of DA and using the standard addition method, measurements were made of DA concentrations in the pharmaceutical preparations and of the recovery rate of the spiked samples. The results are listed in Table 1 .

In this study, we had developed a new composite of rGO-CDs, the composite was used as model film to construct a biosensor for DA detection. It exhibited higher sensibility, wider linear range, lower detection limit and better reproducibility and stability than the bare GCE, CDs/GCE and GO/GCE. In addition, this biosensor also had strong anti-interference to some potential interfering substances. Under the selected conditions the DPV oxidation peak currents showed good linear relationship with DA concentration in the range from 0.01000μM to 450.0μM with the detection limit as 1.5nM. Meanwhile, the rGO-CDs/GCE also was applied to the detection of DA content in its injection with satisfactory results, and the biosensor could keep its activity for at least a month.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the science and technology foundation of the national general administration of quality supervision in China (2012QK053), Fujian province natural science foundation (2012D136) and the science and technology foundation of Fujian provincial bureau quality and technical supervision (NO.FJQI2012029, NO.FJQI 2013108).
